data.title: PBF Energy: Regulatory Relief Pushes Shares Towards Fair Value
data.description:
Shares of PBF Energy Inc. (NYSE:PBF) have been a poor performer this year, losing nearly 10%. Its financial results have been strained by a weak refining environment over the winter, though that has improved. Additionally, it suffered a devastating fire at a refinery earlier this year, and recovery is still underway, though most losses are covered by insurance. That said, the stock has been recovering as refining sentiment has been improving. I last covered shares in August, rating the stock a “Strong Buy,” and this recommendation has played out with the stock adding 35%. Given how much it has run and new developments, now is a good time to revisit PBF.

I believe much of this rally is tied to increased optimism for the sector amid significant pro-industry regulatory actions. In late August, the Trump Administration’s EPA acted on 175 small refinery exemption (“SRE”) petitions, granting 63 in full and 77 partially, a favorable outcome for the industry. Some of these petitions had been pending for over five years, so this clears a significant legal overhang. I also expect an easier and quicker approach to exemptions going forward as well.

Now, in general, a refinery needs less than 75,000 bpd of throughput to qualify for an SRE. Each of PBF’s refineries is above this threshold, so I do not see the company being as much of a beneficiary of the EPA actions as companies like Delek (DK) and CVR Energy (CVI).

However, PBF is still likely to be a beneficiary of this action indirectly, in my view. When a refinery does not use enough renewable feedstock, it needs to purchase “RINs” to offset this shortfall. This can be a large expense for many companies, and PBF has significant RINs exposure. Last year, PBF’s RIN expense was over $500 million, but two years prior, it was $1.2 billion.

RINs pricing can be very volatile, and RINs have become increasingly expensive this year. PBF’s cost “essentially doubled” last quarter. As of Q2, PBF Energy has ~$520 million of accrued RINS obligations. Given the company has just a ~$3.5 billion market capitalization, this is a significant sum. Historically, smaller refiners have been large net buyers of RINs. With their RIN needs going down, their purchases will decline. Plus, much of their historic liability has been wiped away. For instance, CVI needs to buy 300 million fewer RINs.

With these refineries needing to buy fewer RINs, RINs pricing may fall back down, which in turn could reduce PBF’s operating costs. I see PBF free cash flow potentially benefitting by $50-$75 million per year from the EPA’s actions on SRE. At a 10x incremental free cash flow multiple, that is worth about $4-$5 a share, which explains quite a bit of PBF’s rally. This stronger run-rate free cash flow will also ease PBF’s debt concerns, which had been weighing on the stock.

Interestingly, while the stock has done very well, the refining environment has actually worsened a little bit of late. As you can see below, refining margins are much wider today than they were a year ago, but have fallen recently. Last year, the market was struggling with tight crude oil supplies and very strong refining utilization, and these dynamics have changed. Most notably, OPEC+ has been increasing oil production, which has increased the supply of crude oil relative to refined products like gasoline. This dynamic change should keep spreads wider than they were 6-12 months ago.

That said, margins have also fallen in recent weeks after highs hit over the early summer. One reason for this is that the United States has had a very quiet hurricane season. Typically, Gulf Coast refiners have to temporarily shut down some production for storms during the summer, but with no hurricanes hitting the U.S. mainland, refinery throughput has remained strong. Additionally, with sluggish economic growth, demand for gasoline does remain muted, and these factors may be limiting how wide crack spreads can stay. Still, I view levels north of $20-$22 as healthy and supportive of mid-cycle profitability, even if margins are not as strong as they once were.

With gasoline inventories relatively low, I do not see crack spreads crashing below $20 like they did last year, which should enable PBF to report sequentially solid results in Q3 and meaningfully better results over the winter than the company did last year.

There has also been important company-specific news for PBF Energy. On Sept. 30, PBF closed its previously announced sale of terminal assets, including 1.9 million barrels of capacity. This will generate about $175 million in cash proceeds, helping to assist with debt reduction.

In August, the company announced it will be promoting its head of treasury to be its new CFO. While the current CFO is retiring, Karen Davis will rejoin the company’s board of directors. This speaks to the fact that this is an amicable and smooth transition, and as a result, I do not expect there to be any change in the company’s capital allocation policy. The company is also in the midst of a $230 million cost-cutting program, though I remain highly cautious that PBF will be able to lower costs that much on a sustainable basis.

These actions should help to restore its balance sheet. So far this year, its debt load has increased by $900 million. PBF has $591 million of cash and $2.4 billion of debt. Thus far this year, it has faced $250 million of working capital headwinds as it deals with the shutdown of its Martinez refinery. I expect to see some recovery in working capital in H2. Combined with underlying cash flow, insurance payments, and the terminal proceeds, we should see net debt begin to recover by the end of the year. Ultimately, I would like to see PBF operate in the 10%-20% zone. Instead, the company ended Q2 with about 30% net debt to capital.

With their rally, shares are now in the $27-30 fair value range, which I have been using in recent months to value PBF, reflecting about $800-900 million of run-rate EBITDA. I do see the company likely running towards the higher end of my estimate over the medium term, given lower RINs costs. This should ultimately support at least $300 million of free cash flow, or about $30 at an ~8% free cash flow yield, reflecting the low growth and capital-intensive nature of the refining business.

Given how much the stock has run and its smaller RINs benefit, alongside some need for further debt reduction, I feel that PBF’s 35% gain has played out my "Strong Buy" thesis. As such, I am downgrading shares to a “Hold.” This has been an excellent run over just two months, and I would not buy here. I do not see the stock as overpriced, and so I see no rush to sell, though I would never object to investors looking to take a profit after such a large gain.

data.title: SMH: Likely To Keep Outperforming The Market And Move Higher
data.description:
The VanEck Semiconductor ETF (NASDAQ:SMH) has had a phenomenal year, as it has appreciated by roughly 42%, which has outpaced the Nasdaq and the S&P 500 by a country mile. I don’t think it will come as a surprise to anyone that semiconductors have ripped higher throughout the year as the allocation of CapEx continues to increase toward building out data centers. Some of the largest benefactors have been NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), and Broadcom (AVGO), along with Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) and Micron (MU), as the entire stack has seen increased revenues and profitability. There have been many recent developments from Oracle (ORCL) announcing they will be raising $15 billion from a corporate bond sale as they expand their AI data center footprint, and from NVDA, who recently indicated that they plan on investing up to $100 billion in OpenAI to build and deploy 10 gigawatts of AI data centers. While the bears are still trying to convince the investment community that AI is in a bubble similar to the dot-com era in 1999, the reality is that the largest companies in the world are leading the charge, unlike in 1999, when start-ups were fueled by debt from venture capital without generating revenue. I think that the SMH is an interesting pick that provides investors with exposure across the semiconductor space, which will continue to outperform the market indices for the foreseeable future. I’m bullish on SMH and think the total addressable market (TAM) for data centers is only going to expand.

Following up on my previous article about SMH

Since my previous article was published in the middle of June (can be read here), shares of SMH have appreciated by 31.37%, while the S&P 500 has climbed 11.97%. I had discussed that the AI revolution and global chip demand made semiconductors a long-term growth story that would cause shares of SMH to move higher. The massive CapEx spend from big tech, in addition to geopolitical support like the CHIPS Act, is fueling industry expansion, which benefits all companies in the semiconductor value chain, from chips to fabrication. I had discussed that the semiconductor market was estimated to reach $2 trillion in 2032, and this could expand as more information is announced. I am following up with a new article to discuss what has happened since June and why I believe the current upward momentum will continue for SMH. I am bullish on AI and how the semiconductor industry will be impacted over the next several years.

An overview of the VanEck Semiconductor ETF

SMH is an ETF that takes a passive investment approach to building out a portfolio of companies throughout the semiconductor value chain. SMH will allocate at least 80% of its assets to semiconductor companies that range from U.S.-based companies to foreign entities. These companies may include medium-capitalization companies and foreign companies listed on a U.S. exchange. One of the major aspects to keep in mind is that holdings in SMH need to generate at least 50% of its revenues from semiconductors, which means that the fund will be heavy in semiconductors and semiconductor equipment. SMH doesn’t take an active management approach, so it could experience a lot of volatility with the market in the future.

SMH has a 100% weighting toward the technology sector, and the combination of NVDA and TSM makes up 28.53% of the ETF. When investors allocate capital toward SMH, they are effectively indicating that the demand for compute is going to increase and drive more revenue and profitability throughout the chip designers and manufacturers. SMH has $34.46 billion in assets under management (AUM), and investors are getting exposure to equipment suppliers, chip designers, and foundries. SMH is a single-focus ETF, but if the current velocity of spending continues, it may continue to be very lucrative for investors, as there could be enhanced levels of earnings expansion happening over the next several years for the positions within its portfolio.

Why I am still bullish on SMH after a 31.37% return since my previous article

I believe that we’re still in the early stages of the data center expansion due to harnessing the power of AI. In the 20th century, oil was the growth engine for the global economy, and while traditional sources of energy are still important, I believe that semiconductors are the foundational resource of our digital world, as they power everything from cloud data centers to the smartphones in our pockets. I don’t see any indication that technology has ever stood still, as every form of technology has evolved over time. I believe that SMH allows investors to capitalize on the industry leaders at the forefront of this global transformation regarding AI. The rise of Generative AI and Large Language Models (LLMs) has created an unprecedented demand for high-performance Graphics Processing Units (GPUs) and custom AI accelerators. We haven’t even started embedding full self-driving (FSD) or humanoid robotics into commercial use, which could enhance the rate at which data centers are being built to keep up with the future demand for inference training.

There has been a tremendous amount of news over the past several weeks that compounds what we learned from the Q2 conference calls. Microsoft (MSFT) recently delivered a data center announcement, where they are going to spend $4 billion and claim that it will be the most advanced in the world. After that, OpenAI and NVDA announced a strategic partnership to deploy 10 gigawatts of NVDA systems and buy directly from NVDA rather than sourcing their compute from 3rd parties. OpenAI also announced a deal with AMD where they would deploy 6 gigawatts of their next-generation chips starting in the 2nd half of 2026. Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG), Meta Platforms (META), and MSFT finished Q2 having spent $291.35 billion on CapEx over the trailing 12 months (TTM). In Q1 2025, the combined total of these companies CapEx allocation grew 62.35% ($27.61 billion) YoY. In Q2 it accelerated to 66.97% ($35.39 billion) YoY. The rate that the big 4 are spending is increasing, and now OpenAI is becoming a self-sufficient player as they will build and operate their own data centers in addition to purchasing compute from other providers.

The SPDR S&P Semiconductor ETF (XSD) has a modified equal-weighting strategy that tracks the S&P Semiconductor Select Industry Index. This approach gives smaller companies a more significant weighting than in a market cap-weighted fund such as SMH. The Invesco Semiconductor ETF (PSI) uses a quantitative methodology to select and weight its holdings, which is a smart beta approach that goes beyond market cap. This can take factors such as momentum and earnings expansion into consideration. The primary difference for me between SMH, XSD, and PSI is their portfolio construction. SMH focuses on the most liquid semiconductor companies, which leads to a concentrated portfolio towards industry juggernauts. While XSD offers a more diversified approach through its equal weight methodology and PSI takes factors like momentum into consideration, I believe that gaining excess exposure to companies such as NVDA, AMD, and TSM is the better strategy, as these are the companies that should benefit the most from the current infrastructure buildout that is occurring. History has shown us that SMH has dramatically outperformed SPY, QQQ, PSI, and XSD over the past 5 years, and I believe this will continue to occur.

When I look at the top 10 holdings for SMH, which account for 74.09% of the ETF, I get very bullish on the future. The combination of these companies is trading at 63.07 times 2025 earnings and is expected to grow their EPS on average by 126.88%, putting their valuation on 2027 earnings at 24.29 times. If I remove Intel Corporation (INTC) as it’s a massive outlier, the top 9 companies are trading at 35.08 times 2025 earnings and are expected to trade at 23.33 times 2027 earnings as they have an average of 45.61% EPS growth on the horizon. I actually think these EPS estimates could be significantly higher in the coming quarters as the analyst community continues to take their estimates higher after each earnings season. I believe that with all of the news that has been reported, there is a clear path for these companies to become more profitable and take shares of SMH higher.

The risks to investing in SMH

Technology continues to be a winning trade, and companies such as NVDA continue to make all-time highs, but there are risks to investing in SMH. The first risk is the lack of diversification, as SMH needs the CapEx spend to continue at this rate. If spending on data centers slows down and the semiconductor trade cools off, there is no broader market exposure through SMH to fall back on. This could cause shares of SMH to retrace more than the market in the event of a correction. The semiconductor industry is also vulnerable to geopolitical tensions, particularly U.S.-China relations, as its holdings face risks from trade restrictions, tariffs, or regional instability. Everyone should do their own due diligence on the semiconductor industry because investing in SMH is the equivalent of placing all of your chips on black at the roulette table.

Conclusion

The news keeps pouring in, and the United States has approved billions of dollars worth of NVDA chips to be exported through a bilateral AI deal with the UAE. Q3 earnings around the corner, and I believe that it will be bullish for semiconductors because GOOGL, AMZN, and META are likely to announce new CapEx spending plans to compete with OpenAI and MSFT. The U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA) has highlighted that data centers are shaping commercial demand for energy, which is now projected to grow faster than other sectors due to their 24/7 load profiles. I think that this is an indication that the buildout cycle is nowhere near over, and shares of SMH allow investors to gain exposure to a basket of companies in the semiconductor industry that will stand to benefit from the continued data center growth. I think SMH will continue to exceed the market returns, especially when companies such as NVDA receive huge price target increases from $240 to $300.

data.title: Sarepta Therapeutics: A Discounted Valuation Meets Breakthrough Pipeline
data.description:
Sarepta Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ:SRPT) is an interesting case. In recent times, the stock price has been under a lot of scrutiny from the unwanted results of some of its clinical trials. Over the past year, the stock has been down 80% hitting lows in the mid-$10 range. We saw a couple of patient deaths as a result of acute liver failure, which has knocked the stock down in big chunks over 2025. However, after hitting those lows back in late July, the stock has somewhat started to recover, reaching the $23 range we see today. I think this is the start of a turnaround for Sarepta, as they have a couple of nice therapy candidates progressing in their pipeline. The company’s near-term growth should be fueled by SRP-9003. It's a transformative gene therapy for Limb-Girdle Muscular Dystrophy (DMD), which, if successful later down the line, should expand Sarepta’s footprint beyond just Duchenne muscular dystrophy. It’s another rare-disease market with a sizable premium pricing opportunity similar to what they already show. The valuation on Sarepta also remains rather attractive given its $800 million cash figure. Data on the upcoming SRP-9003 study should fall sometime soon, and with a few points to look out for, a positive result could see some upside for the stock as it makes its recovery.

A robust pipeline

Sarepta generates its main revenue via the commercialization of its therapies for Duchenne muscular dystrophy. It is a rare genetic disorder in which boys experience muscular weakness at an early age. However, its main revenue driver is Elevidys, which was the first FDA-approved gene therapy for DMD. As of recent times, Elevidys has become Sarepta’s flagship product and now makes up the majority of its sales growth. The therapy itself has quite a high price point, at around $3.2 million per treatment. And whilst this seems extraordinarily high, this is often the case for one-time genetic disorder treatments. Recent label expansions have allowed the treatment of both ambulatory and non-ambulatory patients.

Aside from Elevidys, Sarepta also earns a substantial sum of its revenue from earlier RNA-based “exon skipping” drugs. These include Exondys 51, Vyondys 53, and Amondys 45. They still remain essential treatments for certain subsets, depending on their genetic mutation, of DMD patients. As most of you know, beyond their product sales, Sarepta has an ongoing collaboration with Roche. It gives them some additional revenue in the form of royalties and milestone payments for ex-U.S. Elevidys rights.

Now, Sarepta’s future growth, of course, depends on its clinical pipeline. They actually have over 40 investigational programs, but I’m going to choose to focus on their clinical-stage drugs, which are showing the most promise. They include several advanced-stage candidates nearing potential approval. By far the most advanced would be SRP-9003, which is a gene therapy for Limb-Girdle Muscular Dystrophy Type 2E. They have already completed enrollment in its Phase 3 EMERGENE study. We should expect the data sometime in 2025.

For me, their biggest near-term catalyst would have to be SRP-9003, purely because of the fact that it would expand Sarepta’s presence way beyond Duchenne into another group of muscular dystrophies. Elsewhere, the company is moving forward with its next-generation siRNA therapies, SRP-1001, tipped ot be for Facioscapulohumeral Muscular Dystrophy. SRP-1003, for Myotonic Dystrophy Type 1, is also being progressed. Both of these candidates are in Phase 1/2 trials via their collaboration with Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (ARWR). These programs will be key in the near term as they are essentially Sarepta’s diversification into gene-silencing platforms. As they grow, they will require a gene-splicing platform to complement their gene therapy business. I put the most important therapies to focus on in the table below to get a sense of their clinical stage and what to look out for next.

2Q25 Review

For 2Q25, the company saw roughly $611.1 million in total revenue, up substantially from $362.9 million at the same time last year. It's a 68% year-over-year growth jump. On a non-GAAP adjusted basis, earnings per share came in at about $2.02, which was well above consensus expectations of $1.11. It's also sharply higher than the $0.07 EPS figure we saw a year ago. The company also reported some good operating profitability on both GAAP and a non-GAAP basis. In terms of cash flow, the quarter was also positive, with the company’s cash, cash equivalents, and investments increasing by roughly $202.8 million compared to the prior quarter. If we turn to costs, R&D plus SG&A (excluding non-cash items) rose a bit, but we can put that largely down to expanded clinical and collaboration activity.

As of last quarter, Sarepta held approximately $850 million in cash. As per their 10-Q, they list “total cash, cash equivalents and restricted cash” at about $526 million, but when you include the short- and long-term investments, the broader liquidity figure jumps to that $850 million mark. The big news out of last earnings was the restructuring plan, which they hope will reduce costs by about $400 million annually starting in 2025. They intend to refocus their pipeline and cut down some manufacturing commitments.

SRP-9003

As many investors already know and follow quite closely, SRP-9003 is the company's next promising near-term growth driver following Elevidys. It’s actually built on the same AAVrh74 vector platform, which is already used successfully in its Duchenne muscular dystrophy program. Now, what SRP-9003 does is that it delivers a functional copy of the β-sarcoglycan gene, which is the gene mutated in Limb-Girdle Muscular Dystrophy, also called LGMDR4. The candidate has completed enrollment in the Phase 3 EMERGENE trial. This trial is meant to confirm the efficacy and any safety signals observed in earlier studies.

Now, we should expect the top-line results sometime in late 2025 or early 2026. And if results show promise, we can expect the company to submit a Biologics License Application to the FDA soon after. The trial’s main endpoints focused on β-sarcoglycan protein expression in muscle tissue, while other endpoints measured functional improvement over time. This included any motor strength and endurance. So investors will keep an eye out for that protein expression level and any improvement in motor strength. However, what we do have to give us an indication is early-phase data from the SRP-9003 program. It showed robust and sustained expression of β-sarcoglycan and thus improved functional outcomes. It also displayed a quite acceptable safety profile, which gave investors reason for optimism heading into the main readouts.

I expect the data press release to substantially move the stock in either direction depending on the outcome, so here's what to really focus on. For investors, the key things will be the magnitude and durability of the protein expression, the functional gains versus the baseline, and hopefully the absence of any major safety concerns. On the safety side of things, we don’t want to see things like liver toxicity or immune reactions. These are the kind of side effects that can really halt a pipeline candidate. We saw issues like this that have recently affected the company’s DMD gene therapy, Elevidys. The key to all this data is also keeping regulators happy. They’ll also scrutinize manufacturing consistency, given that AAV-based therapies must demonstrate lot-to-lot reliability, so the consistency of drug production across different batches. As for a timeline, I’m anticipating that positive Phase 3 data could pretty quickly translate into regulatory approval. This would set up SRP-9003 nicely as a second blockbuster gene therapy for Sarepta.

As for what's on offer, analysts are estimating the TAM for LGMD2E to fall somewhere just below 5,000 patients globally, since the incidence rate is about 1 in 200,000 to 1 in 350,000. After all, it’s only a small but highly lucrative rare-disease niche. In terms of price points, it's still very early days, but as I mentioned earlier with genetherapies, we usually see very high one-off prices. We should see a premium somewhat similar to Elevidys. So, a likely premium one-time price is in the $2 to $3 million range. Importantly, Sarepta is already commercially equipped to launch SRP-9003 efficiently, as it would follow a very similar marketing strategy to Elevidys.

Valuation

Sarepta is trading right now in the low $23 range. It holds a market cap of about $2.27 billion and an enterprise value of $2.84 billion. In terms of valuation, on a forward basis, the EV to sales ratio sits at 1.39x and the price to sales at 1.12x. Both are well below the sector medians of 3.80x and 4.28x, respectively. So Sarepta is being priced at quite a discount to peers, despite the company having a very advanced gene therapy pipeline in neuromuscular diseases. The forward price-to-book ratio is at 2.02x and also sits below the sector median of 2.99x, which tells us the stock is trading below the replacement value of its assets. Forward-looking price-to-earnings estimates for 2026 through 2027 are at 10.35x and 13.56x. These suggest a fairly modest multiple relative to future growth potential. I say modest here as we should consider the near-term catalysts from Elevidys and SRP-9003. EV to EBITDA is, of course, extremely high at 70.66x on a TTM basis. This is very typical for clinical-stage companies with hefty investments into R&D. However, for me, this is offset by the fact that Sarepta has a pretty strong cash position of $800 million.

Risks

The biggest here would be clinical and regulatory uncertainty around SRP-9003. I touched on safety concerns before, and we all saw it back in June. The company reported a second patient death as a result of acute liver failure. When nasty side effects like these are reported, it often hurts small pharma stocks a lot more than usual. Each time positive news is released on a drug stage or management hints towards success in earning the new market it begins to price in a positive data outcome from the upcoming trials. However, when we see harsh safety concerns, all those positives get priced out in one go, so we often see sharp sell-offs. Think of this also from an FDA point of view, they view the overall package of a candidate starting from the beginning and take all data points of studies leading into a decision. So with multiple patient deaths, they would, of course, be more inclined to extend or request more data later down the line.

Conclusion

So next to watch out for would be the SRP-9003 Phase 3 data that we’re all expecting soon. Management originally tipped it to come sometime in mid-2025. In terms of data, focus on the β-sarcoglycan expression, any functional gains, and those safety signals, such as liver toxicity. We may also get updates on Sarepta’s siRNA pipeline (SRP-1001 and SRP-1003) if there is a full press release when the data comes. Just before earnings, we have the World Muscle Society Congress in October to look forward to; we should expect an update on SGCB expression and safety then, which I'll be watching. 3Q25 earnings are also coming up soon and should fall in early November.

data.title: Skillz Should Consider An Alternative To Its Current Turnaround Plan
data.description:
Last December, I took a critical view of Skillz's (NYSE:SKLZ). In a nutshell I argued that the company, which operates a mobile platform featuring real-money skill-based competition games, had a questionable path to profitability.

Furthermore, I argued that, despite looking like a bargain on paper, the company's tangible book value was likely not as high as it appeared, due to much of it being represented by illiquid investments.

However, while I thought all signs pointed to lower prices for SKLZ stock, Mr. Market thought differently. So far this year, investors have become more confident in the company's turnaround potential. As a result, shares have soared by more than 35%.

Taking a second look at Skillz, I'll concede that the company has made some operational progress. Also, there may be more potential value here than I assumed before.

That said, it still appears chancy that this turnaround will be flawlessly executed. Instead of the current strategy, I think management should consider an alterative that could utilize the company's large cash position, as well as a massive "hidden asset." With this, I wouldn't immediately jump into a position, but I would say SKLZ is worth keeping on your watchlist. Here's why.

SKLZ Stock: Recap and Recent Developments

Skillz shares experienced sideways price performance during the first few months of the year, in line with the broad market. Some company-specific factors, however, did play a role in price performance.

Alongside disappointing Q1 2025 results (discussed further below), the company also received a compliance notice from the New York Stock Exchange, due to delays in filing its annual 10-K. The notice called for Skillz to file its 10-K with the SEC within six months of the compliance notice issue date, which was March 17, 2025.

That said, since April, shares have moved higher in tandem with the broad market meltup that has occurred since concerns about tariff hikes and other macro factors have subsided. Alongside this, there have been some improvements to Skillz's fundamentals since the start of the year.

As seen in results for Q1 2025, the same issue plaguing the company in 2024 hadn't fully gone away at the start of this year. That is, efforts to normalize marketing spend while retaining as large of a user base as possible remained challenging, as revenue declines exceeded reduced marketing spend.

However, as seen in Skillz's Q2 2025 results, perhaps the company is starting to make some headway with this strategy. During the quarter ending June 30, 2025, revenue came in at $27.4 million, up 8.3% from the prior year, and up 30% on a sequential, or quarter-over-quarter, basis. With sales and marketing expenses, along with G&A expenses, continuing to drop, the company reported a significant narrowing in operating losses, which came in at just $6.6 million.

Excluding a one-time item from Q2 2024 (a $46 million litigation settlement gain), this represented big improvement compared to the prior year's quarter, not to mention Q1, where operating losses, excluding a separate litigation gain of $7.5 million, came in at around $20.7 million.

With these results, it's no surprise SKLZ stock climbed to new multi-year highs after these results hit the street on Aug. 7. Since then, shares have held steady between $7 and $8 per share. While not for certain, investors may be waiting for the company to unveil improved results yet again in November, when Skillz next reports quarterly earnings.

However, this relative stability also comes despite a recent development emerging that may impact operating performance moving forward. On Aug. 29, Skillz disclosed that Tether Studios, developers of two key games on Skillz's platform, Solitaire Cube and 21 Blitz, announced that it was terminating its agreements with the company, effective Sep. 1, 2025.

The main agreement between the two companies is that Tether licenses Skillz's monetization software, and both companies split the revenue generated from the games. Skillz is fighting the termination, with the company filing a suit in Delaware Chancery court immediately after the termination announcement.

Irrespective of whether Skillz proves successful in avoiding this termination, or even if Skillz ends up landing another legal windfall from its latest litigation efforts, the risk/reward proposition with this remains very questionable, especially after this year's surge in price.

Valuation

Still unprofitable on not just GAAP basis, but even on an adjusted EBITDA basis, it is tough to value SKLZ stock based on traditional valuation metrics like P/E or EV/EBITDA. Again, one could take a balance sheet-based valuation approach, and try to estimate Skillz's underlying value based on figures like tangible book value.

However, as before, there are caveats with valuing the company this way. For one, as seen from fiscal results (which continue to be "preliminary," mind you, as Skillz has remained delinquent in both annual and quarterly SEC filings), tangible book value has continued to decline in recent quarters.

As of Dec. 31, 2024, tangible book value was at $171.6 million, or around $10.28 per share. As of June 30, 2025, tangible book value was down by $31.2 million, coming in at $140.4 million, or $9.18 per share. If operating cash burn of around $10 million to $20 million for another quarter or two, assuming shares held steady, this tangible book discount would fully disappear.

Second, while Skillz, even after this year's rally, still trades at a double-digit discount to tangible book, a large portion of tangible book value continues to consist of what are classified as "long-term investments."

As I detailed in my last SKLZ stock write-up, these "long-term investments," still on the books at $52.8 million as they were at the end of last year, likely consist mainly of the company's $50 million investment in Exit Games, one of its mobile game developer partners.

It remains unclear how much of Exit Games this company actually owns, and valuing Exit Games remains a challenge as well. A cursory search still brings up the $200 million-$300 million figure I cited previously as a possible enterprise value for this entity.

In short, with a balance sheet-based approach perhaps also not a good way to value SKLZ, maybe the best way to estimate how profitable SKLZ's platform could be, if operating expenses are finally right-sized to match the scale of the business.

Taking at look at margins of other mobile gaming companies, like East Side Games Group (OTCQB:EAGRF), PLAYSTUDIOS (MYPS), and Playtika (PLTK), I believe it's reasonable to say that, at best, Skillz could achieve EBITDA margins of around 10% range.

More similar in scale to EAGRF and MYPS than Playtika, Skillz could probably achieve margins in line with these competitors. Over the past twelve months, EAGRF's EBITDA margins have been around 7.3% on $58.4 million in revenue, while MYPS's EBITDA margins have been around 12.6% on $261.1 million in revenue.

Giving Skillz the benefit of the doubt, assuming that last quarter's top-line figure will repeat itself in the coming quarters, let's presume that the company's annualized revenue moving forward comes out to around $108 million. At 10% EBITDA margins, EBITDA could come out to around $10.8 million.

On the flip-side, however, it's debatable whether this business, if profitable, could achieve that high of a valuation. EAGRF currently has an EV/EBITDA ratio of just 4.1, while MYPS trades at an EV/EBITDA ratio below 1. Then again, valuation for both companies may be deceptively low, as EBITDA is inclusive of items that, while capitalized, should arguably be included as operating expenses, such as game licensing costs.

Since Skillz's revenue model works differently, where it's licensing its monetization platform to developers, rather than licensing games from developers, a higher valuation is likely justified. Even then, however, if growth prospects remain questionable, an EBITDA multiple in the high single-digits (let's call it 7x to 8x EBITDA) may be the most reasonable EBITDA a steadily-profitable Skillz could achieve.

With this in mind, let's estimate that the potential value of Skillz's operating business could end up being around $75.6 million to $86.4 million. To come up with a concrete value for the business, let's average these numbers, then round them down to the closest $5 million. That takes us to $80 million.

Yes, at first, this may suggest that there is indeed some upside potential with SKLZ, even at current prices. Add to the $80 million SKLZ's war chest of cash ($228.7 million) plus the long-term investments ($52.8 million), as well as its Las Vegas, NV headquarters building (purchased in 2023 for $11.5 million), and we get a gross value of $373 million.

Subtracting long-term debt ($126.6 million) and other current liabilities ($52.1 million), we get a "sum of the parts" valuation of $194.3 million, or around $12.67 per share, around 63.8% above the current stock price.

However, while that upside potential may sound like enough, it may not be enough. Why? Take into account execution risk, plus the prospect of further cash burn, and once again, SKLZ stock doesn't appear as much of a bargain as it does based on either book value or more creative valuation methods.

A Possible Alternative Route for Skillz

Let's be clear: the following is going to be my idea of what Skillz should do, in lieu of continuing down the path of making its platform "work." I wouldn't buy SKLZ for the possibility of such an alternative to be considered, but I think it's great food for thought. Other busted growth stories, like ContextLogic Holdings (OTC:LOGC), have taken a similar alternative route.

As was the case at the end of 2024, Skillz remains somewhat of a "melting ice cube," but considerable value remains. Instead of throwing the dice a few more times, running down the war chest in the process, what if Skillz decided to throw in the towel, either by winding down Skillz, or by somehow selling it/merging it with another mobile gaming company?

In its current state, the operating business may not be able to fetch much in terms of acquisition price, but I wouldn't rule out the potential for a deep-pocketed buyer to be willing to buy it for a symbolic $1, in exchange for assuming its liabilities and likely near-term operating losses.

Next, Skillz could work to sell off the Las Vegas headquarters building, as well as use its cash position to pay off outstanding debt. Liquidating in this manner, the company could wind up with around $113.6 million in cash, while still retaining the "long-term investments."

Even if Skillz could only get 50 cents on the dollar for its investments in Epic Games and other mobile developers, that's still around $26.4 million. Add that to the net cash position, and SKLZ would be sitting on $140 million free and clear, a figure that's around 18% above the current market cap.

Now, instead of distributing this to shareholders in the form of a liquidation dividend, how about this: alongside the value of tangible assets like cash and real estate, Skillz has another "hidden asset," its net operating loss (NOL) carryforwards from past years of extreme unprofitability.

As of Dec. 31, 2024, the company's U.S. Federal NOLs total $611.5 million, a figure that has likely increased in subsequent quarters. Utilizing these via the purchase of the profitable business could in theory be a better strategy for maximizing shareholder value.

In fact, it may not necessarily have to be a matter of "either/or," with the NOLs, plus the high amount of net cash, maybe Skillz has the means to acquire a profitable mobile gaming company, consolidating operations with Skillz, resulting in cost/growth synergies.

Bottom Line on SKLZ Stock

Looking back, I definitely glossed over much of the potential at hand with Skillz. Now that the company has successfully brought operating losses to more acceptable levels for at least fiscal quarter, there may be greater reason for hope in this company's future prospects.

That said, the turnaround potential has become much more factored into SKLZ's valuation. At the same time, uncertainty runs high whether the company will be successful at normalizing its cost structure, bringing it to a point of profitability.

While it's nice to theorize how this company could turn itself into a NOL shell, possibly resulting in greater, less risky gains than its current turnaround strategy, once again it makes little sense to buy in now, in the hopes this alternative is even considered.

For now, I'm keeping an eye on SKLZ stock. Shares could still unexpectedly experience another round of volatility, and not just due to the next quarterly earnings release falling short of expectations.

The company's compliance issue is not yet resolved. In a recent 8-K filing, Skillz noted that it has presented a compliance plan to the NYSE, and now has until Dec. 17, 2025 to catch up with its past-due 10-K and 10-Q filings, in order to avoid delisting. It is concerning that these reports remain overdue, and further delays could once again weigh on shares.

data.title: Albany International Corp Is A 'Show Me' Story
data.description:
As a value investor, I prioritize the price at which shares of a company are trading relative to earnings and cash flows above almost anything else. But one of the pitfalls that value investors run into is that they sometimes overlook other important aspects. For instance, Albany International Corp (NYSE:AIN) is a rather interesting business with a history of revenue growth over the last few years. This year, sales have declined. But even more problematic than that, I would argue, is the fact that profitability has been all over the map, with cash flows for the company falling over the last couple of years now. Long term, the business is well positioned to create shareholder value. And if management is correct about what this year will bring, there could be some nice upside. But until we see some real progress, I would argue that taking a more cautious approach and rating it a "Hold" is the appropriate course of action.

Digging into Albany International

According to the management team at Albany International, the company operates as a major player in the advanced textiles and materials processing space. They design and manufacture high performance engineered fabrics and composite components and assemblies that are used in a wide array of industries. Examples here include the paper, industrial manufacturing, and aerospace markets. To really understand the company, however, I think it would be important to dig into each of its operating segments.

The largest of these currently is the Machine Clothing segment, which last year was responsible for 60.9% of the firm's overall revenue. Through this unit, the company produces and sells certain fabrics and high-speed process belts used in the production of paper products. It also provides consumable permeable and impermeable belts used in the production of paper, paper board, tissue, pulp, and more. And on top of this, it also provides customers with engineered fabric products for use in things like fiber cement.

The other operating segment is the Albany Engineered Composites segment. This part of the company provides certain technology solutions and operates as a producer of engineered components, assemblies, and more, for the aerospace and defense markets. Interestingly, this segment caters to Safran Group, which happens to be the partner of GE Aerospace (GE) in its production of the LEAP engine that is used in many of the major aircraft that take to the skies today.

Over the last few years, management has done a fine job of growing the company's top line. Revenue went from $1.03 billion in 2022 to $1.23 billion in 2024. This growth in revenue was driven by both of its operating segments. For starters, the Machine Clothing business reported a jump in revenue from $609.5 million to $749.9 million. According to management, this rise in sales was driven by a couple of factors. There seems to be greater demand for tissue, pulp, and engineered fabrics. But another seems to be the firm's purchase of Heimbach, which accounted for $141.6 million of revenue last year but only $51.2 million a year before that. For those not familiar with the enterprise, in August 2023, Albany International purchased Heimbach, which at the time was a privately held producer of paper machine clothing and technical textiles. This cost shareholders $133.5 million.

At the same time, we did see growth when it came to the Albany Engineered Composites business. Between 2022 and 2024, its revenue expanded from $425.4 million to $480.7 million. Based on the data provided, this rise in revenue came almost entirely from the 2022 to 2023 fiscal years. This is not surprising to me in the least bit. As I have detailed in other articles about GE Aerospace, like this one and this one, that business is growing rapidly. And a big driver of its expansion over the last several years has been the LEAP engine. After the COVID-19 pandemic, air travel quickly recovered. And today, it is higher than it has ever been. This would naturally create demand for the types of offerings that Albany International is responsible for.

Even though the top line for the company has improved consistently, the same cannot be said of the bottom line. After seeing net income rise from $95.8 million to $111.1 million from 2022 to 2023, there was a decline to $87.6 million last year. As the first chart in this article illustrates, other profitability metrics have been rather volatile. It is true that operating cash flow grew consistently over the last three completed fiscal years. But once we adjust for changes in working capital, we actually get consistent declines, with a drop from $203.6 million to $169.7 million. Meanwhile, after peaking at $265.1 million in 2023, EBITDA for the company dropped to $232 million last year.

Normally, I would get into what caused this weakness. And I certainly will. But before I get into that, I think covering results for 2025 would be a wise idea. In the chart above, you can see financial performance for the first half of 2025 compared to the same time last year. Revenue actually fell rather meaningfully from $645.3 million to $600.2 million. This decline was driven by weakness across both operating segments.

For starters, the Machine Clothing segment reported a drop in revenue from $378.8 million to $355.6 million. According to management, the company struggled with decreased demand in Asia and unplanned equipment downtime in one of its production facilities that negatively affected revenue. When it came to the Albany Engineered Composites segment, meanwhile, revenue dropped from $266.5 million to $244.6 million. This one was surprising to me considering the aerospace and defense market. But according to management, there were reductions associated with some commercial and space programs that more than offset strength in the defense category.

The good news, I guess, is that this was really caused by "estimate at completion" adjustments. In essence, the company came to the conclusion that some of its contracted revenue is more expensive than they expected it to be. This caused them to revise lower, over time, how much revenue could be recognized during that window of time. The positive thing here is that management did say that new projects are showing signs of growth.

Regardless of the reason, profitability for the company did take a hit, with net income falling from $51.9 million to $26.5 million. Other profitability metrics worsened without exception. Operating cash flow, as an example, plunged from $93 million to $34.8 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a fall from $101.7 million to $77.7 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business dropped from $128.5 million to $107.6 million.

As promised, I think it is appropriate to talk about what has caused the declines in profitability and cash flows recently. The drop in revenue this year is certainly to blame for some of it. But as the chart above illustrates, the cost of goods sold reported by the company and its selling, general, and administrative costs, all in relation to revenue, increased over time. Unfortunately, on the cost of a good side of things, a lot of that pain appears to be attributable to the firm's purchase of Heimbach. The selling, general, and administrative costs are more nuanced. These declined from 18.72% of sales in 2023 to 17.14% last year.

That, according to management, seems to be thanks to greater global information system costs, higher professional and personnel-related expenses, foreign currency fluctuations, and Heimbach. Certain asset remeasurements, as well as consulting expenses and inflationary pressures that management was unable to pass on to customers, all worked to negatively affect general and administrative costs this year relative to last year. And as for the gross profit margin for the company, the worsening that we saw this year can be chalked up in large part to the aforementioned cumulative charges associated with long-term contracts that I mentioned already.

Interestingly, management does seem optimistic about this year. Even though revenue is expected to come in at between $1.165 billion and $1.265 billion, which at the midpoint would represent a decline of 1.3% compared to what was seen last year, they anticipate EBITDA of around $250 million. That would be up from the $232 million reported the previous year. If we see adjusted operating cash flow rise at the same rate, we would get a reading for it this year of $182.9 million. I understand that a lot of the issues that the company saw in the first half of this year were attributable to certain one-time events. On top of this, management has been working to cut costs in key areas. In fact, during the second quarter of this year, the firm decided to close two additional facilities to cut costs.

In the chart above, you can see what this would mean for the company from a valuation standpoint. Shares of the company do look attractively priced. That is especially true on a forward basis. Relative to other similar firms, I would argue that shares are cheap as well. In the table below, I compared it to five other enterprises of a similar nature. On a price-to-operating cash flow basis, two of the five firms ended up being cheaper than it is. And on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, only one of the companies was cheaper.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Albany International Corp 8.6 7.7
Kennametal (KMT) 8.0 7.4
Tennant Company (TNC) 17.6 10.0
Helios Technologies (HLIO) 13.8 15.4
Hillman Solutions Corp (HLMN) 11.7 11.0
Hillenbrand (HI) 14.5 8.9

Takeaway

To be honest with you, Albany International doesn't look bad. But it also doesn't look great either. The mixed financial performance that the company has seen is disappointing, to say the least. Admittedly, shares of the company are cheap on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar enterprises. But at the same time, I remain cautious because I think we need to see some real progress before we can say that the worst is behind us. The fact of the matter is that the company has a history of declining cash flows even at a time when revenue is rising. And until management can get that under control, I think that taking a more cautious approach with a "Hold" rating is the right choice.

data.title: AMD's Missing (Ultra Accelerator) Link
data.description:
On October 6, 2025, Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD) and OpenAI announced a strategic partnership in which OpenAI will purchase up to 6 gigawatts of AI infrastructure based on AMD products. The first gigawatt is due to be delivered by the second half of 2026 and features AMD GPU accelerators and what the announcement terms "rack-scale AI solutions". Rack scale integration of GPUs into something that performs like a single GPU has become essential as the needs of AI models have outgrown even the largest GPU. However, AMD lacks a crucial element of that rack scale integration, which is still in development by a consortium that AMD leads, the Ultra Accelerator Link consortium. Failure to develop a UALink switch with acceptable performance would likely end the partnership with OpenAI.

AMD's partnership with OpenAI faces a substantial performance risk

The training of AI models has long required many tens or hundreds of GPU accelerators, but until the development of so-called "reasoning" AI models, the computational demands of inference were modest. Even now, small pre-trained models can be run on PCs, and even smartphones.

The reasoning models require a lot more compute power, and commercial models like ChatGPT require hundreds or even thousands of times the compute power of conventional AI. This has made necessary the development of "rack scale" integration of GPUs into something approaching a single gigantic GPU.

This is achieved through a type of networking pioneered by AMD's rival, Nvidia (NVDA), based on Nvidia's NVLink Switch. Whereas high performance data center fiber optic networks operate at 800 gigabits/sec, NVLink operates at 1.8 terabytes/sec.

The wording of the partnership agreement with OpenAI makes clear that AMD is expected to supply a rack-scale AI processing solution:

Under this definitive agreement, OpenAI will work with AMD as a core strategic compute partner to drive large-scale deployments of AMD technology starting with the AMD Instinct MI450 series and rack-scale AI solutions and extending to future generations.

The need for "scale up" networking to combine multiple GPU accelerators is now taken for granted, as is the continued need for "scale out" fiber optic networking in the data center. However, AMD's experience with scale up was limited to its version of a point-to-point high speed data link that could only scale up a cluster of around 8 GPUs.

Recognizing that it was disadvantaged in scale-up, AMD has incentivized OpenAI to use its technology through the offer of 160 million shares of AMD common stock at a purchase price of $0.01/share, essentially free. This part of the agreement is specified in an SEC 8K filed on the day of the announcement.

The purchase warrant issued to OpenAI vests in a series of tranches of unspecified size up to the 160 million share limit. The first tranche is expected to vest when AMD delivers the first 1 gigawatt of AI compute by the end of 2026. The vesting of the warrant is tied to unspecified commercial and technical milestones.

Although also not specified, OpenAI must have performance requirements for the system that AMD is expected to provide. Obviously, OpenAI would not want to provide a gigawatt of power to a system that didn't meet their requirements.

The announcement states that OpenAI's commitment to buy the first gigawatt of AI compute from AMD is "binding", but there appears to be no penalty for OpenAI if it backs out of the deal, other than not getting the free AMD shares of the first and subsequent tranches.

Almost certainly, AMD's Instinct GPUs will meet the requirements of the system. The Instinct MI300 chips have already achieved rough parity with Nvidia Blackwell, and the next gen Instinct MI450 chips should be even better.

But in the scale-up era of AI, individual GPU performance is no longer an important discriminator. What counts is aggregate performance of dozens or more GPUs. This is where the risk is for AMD.

AMD is counting on the Ultra Accelerator Link consortium to produce the equivalent of the NVLink Switch. But the UALink consortium was only formed about a year ago and to date has only produced a version 1.0 of the UALink spec.

AMD and its partners in the consortium have yet to show any working hardware, even at the prototype level, which is not surprising considering the early stage of UALink development. The consortium now has about a year to come up with reliable, proven hardware which meets OpenAI's requirements.

Considering that Nvidia is already on its third generation of NVLink Switch and plans to deliver the fourth generation next year with Vera Rubin, it will be difficult for AMD and its partners to deliver a competitive product. OpenAI may still decide that even with the effective subsidy provided by the warrant, AMD's hardware isn't worth the total cost of ownership. A gigawatt of power isn't cheap.

AMD forms the Ultra Accelerator Consortium in response to Blackwell rack scale integration

The UALink Consortium was announced on May 30, 2024, a little over two months after Nvidia had unveiled its Blackwell GPU accelerator and the DGX NVL72 rack system at its GPU Technology Conference (GTC 24). The NVL72 system integrated 72 separate Blackwells into what was claimed to be "1 Giant GPU":

The claim that NVL72 functions as a single GPU should be taken with a big grain of salt, but it did represent an unprecedented level of scale up, enabled by Nvidia's latest generation of NVLink Switch.

At CEO Lisa Su's Computex Keynote on June 2, 2024, she extolled the virtues of UA Link:

It was clear from the timing of the announcement and the lack of specifics that this was in response to DGX NVL72. AMD had realized belatedly that it needed something comparable to NVLink Switch, since the competition for AI infrastructure was moving beyond the individual accelerator, or even small clusters of them.

Su promised that within a year the UALink consortium would produce the first version of its UALink specification. However, it was indicative of the haste with which the consortium was assembled that it wouldn't even be incorporated until October 29, 2024.

The consortium did indeed release its V1.0 specification on April 8 of this year. At an "Advancing AI" special event on June 13, 2025, AMD announced the arrival of the UALink specification and previewed future systems that would use it.

However, when key elements of these systems were shown, there was only a placeholder for UALink:

I'm reasonably certain that if there had been even prototype hardware to show, there would have been a picture of it. AMD also brought on representatives from Astera Labs and Marvell who expressed their support for UALink. Presumably, these companies would build UALink hardware, and while they avowed plans to build UALink products, neither announced specific products or timetables for them.

AMD left it unspecified when exactly UALink hardware would arrive, but implied that it would be ready in 2026 when the company intends to deliver its Helios rack scale system:

AMD Helios is intended to be competitive with Nvidia's Vera Rubin and is clearly the basis of what AMD intends to deliver to OpenAI:

Some history of Nvidia's NVLink Switch

Helios represents a "great leap forward" strategy, and as such it is fraught with risk. Unlike the way it's often depicted in movies, technology development in the commercial sector tends to occur small increments.

While AMD and its partners are just starting on their first generation of UALink, Nvidia is on its third generation of NVLink Switch, based on version 5 of NVLink:

When Nvidia delivers Vera Rubin in 2026, NVLink 6 bandwidth will double again to 3600 GB/sec. The total aggregate bandwidth shown above represents the product of the link bandwidth times the maximum number of NVLinks that can be supported, which is 576 for NVLink 5 Switches.

The current generation of Blackwell rack scale systems are based on NVLink 5 Switches, which Nvidia unveiled at GTC 2024:

Each NVL72 system uses 9 such switches to interconnect the 72 Blackwell GPUs via NVLink. NVLink Switch represents a multigenerational, multi-year development effort that the UALink consortium is attempting to catch up to in the space of about a year, using the UALink spec as a point of departure.

Astera Labs is the most likely hardware provider for UALink but has no specific products yet for UALink. They have an interesting video about what they call AI Infrastructure 2.0.

What's remarkable about the video is that they present the idea that "AI has outgrown the server" as if it were a new unique insight. Nvidia had anticipated this well before Blackwell rack systems were unveiled last year at GTC 24.

In the video, Astera also claims that AI Infrastructure 2.0 can only be deployed through open standards such as UALink. This seems specious on its face, since Nvidia is already shipping about a thousand Blackwell rack systems per week.

AMD and its partners have tried very hard to make the case for "open ecosystems" in software and hardware, but the harsh reality is that AMD is forced to rely on open sources because it can't afford to undertake the software and hardware developments on its own.

And Nvidia may have obviated many of the claimed benefits of open source for UALink by making NVLink an open standard. At Computex 2025, Nvidia announced NVLink Fusion, which allows any chip maker to incorporate NVLink interfaces into their chips for free:

Note that Astera and Marvell are listed as partners.

Clearly, Nvidia didn't do this out of the goodness of their hearts. If an NVLink user wants to scale up beyond a few devices, they'll need NVLink Switches from Nvidia. But at least, NVLink users have assurance that the NVLink Switch hardware actually exists.

Investor takeaways: what true technology leadership looks like

The word leadership figures prominently in CEO Lisa Su's presentations, as it did at the "Advancing AI" keynote this year. But accepting the claim requires some short term memory loss. AMD is still playing catch up with Nvidia's rack scale AI compute platforms.

Clearly, AMD's strategy is not to lead, but rather to follow in Nvidia's footsteps. This has the advantage that it spares AMD considerable expense. It allows AMD to focus its limited development budget on proven technology advances rather than chasing down dead ends.

AMD did this with ray tracing in games, and now it's doing the same thing with UALink and rack scale integration. But this imposes a lag time on AMD's products. While AMD is still waiting for UALink hardware to be developed, Nvidia is delivering about 1000 GB300 NVL72 rack systems per week, according their fiscal 2026 Q2 earnings report.

Nvidia's ability to anticipate future technology trends and deliver products that anticipate customer needs has led to huge growth in Nvidia's data center software and equipment market share, as shown in the graphic below:

The chart is based on reported revenue for identified "Data Center" or related segments in company quarterly reports. Here I've excluded companies that merely resell products from other companies and included the major original software and equipment vendors that serve the data center market.

Nvidia's share growth has been unprecedented, and this is what true leadership looks like. AMD has managed some growth in share, mostly at the expense of CPU rival Intel.

In terms of absolute quarterly revenue, the data center market has grown to nearly $90 billion per quarter:

Some caveats regarding the charts above. In some cases, reporting of data center-related revenue is not perfectly "clean". For instance, IBM (IBM) reports revenue under Infrastructure, but Infrastructure sales don't consist exclusively of IBM specific hardware such as mainframes but may also include some third party hardware such as GPUs from Nvidia or AMD.

With no ability to disaggregate their sales, I've decided to simply include their reported Infrastructure and Software revenue. This overstates their market share, but it builds some conservatism into the analysis.

Likewise, Broadcom's (AVGO) reporting doesn't specify an end market such as the data center. Their relevant reporting categories are Semiconductors and Infrastructure Software (Broadcom owns VM Ware). Most of Semiconductor revenue is for data center applications but also includes things like WiFi chips.

Once again, rather than ignore Broadcom's participation in the data center and AI, I'm simply including all reported revenue from the relevant categories. This also overstates Broadcom's share.

In compiling the data for the charts, I may have omitted some smaller companies. If the reader believes I've missed an important data center supplier (OEM), please let me know in the comments.

Often, we hear that AI could be a bubble, and that's certainly possible. If it is, Nvidia will have scooped up most of the AI revenue opportunity, once again, by virtue of its true leadership. And AMD will have relatively little to show for its strategy of following in Nvidia's footsteps.

So, in addition to the technical risk associated with the OpenAI partnership, there is also the risk that AI demand could simply deflate, leaving OpenAI with no need for gigawatts of AI compute.

I'm still amazed that we're now calculating AI compute in terms of gigawatts. The average nuclear power plant produces a few gigawatts. It's an open question whether we even have sufficient power to realize OpenAi's ambitions.

AMD investors often argue that AMD can still achieve substantial growth following Nvidia's lead. I think that's a reasonable argument, if you ignore the risks of falling too far behind. AMD has very credible products in CPUs and GPUs for the data center, so I consider them a respectable Hold.

But I wouldn't be a buyer of AMD for the simple fact that there are better investments. I generally only invest in technology industry leaders, and clearly the industry leader here is not AMD.

data.title: JOYY: Turning Bullish On Attractive Yield, Advertising (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I'm raising JOYY Inc.'s (NASDAQ:JOYY) rating from "Hold" to "Buy." In my view, its generous cash distributions and solid business diversification progress should be rewarded with higher valuations.

My prior May 30, 2024, update highlighted the mixed read-across from its 1Q24 financials.

JOYY Offers A 10% Yield

The firm has already shared $0.14 billion of its cash with stockholders in 1H25 as per S&P Capital IQ data.

JOYY participated in Goldman Sachs's (GS) investor event last month, and emphasized its promise of attaining key capital return goals. This information was obtained from GS's Sept. 8 report (inaccessible in public) titled "Asia Leaders Conference 2025."

Earlier in March this year, it revealed a "dividend policy for the following three years," with its "quarterly dividend" being "set at a fixed amount of approximately $50 million." The company also initiated a "share repurchase program" where it "may repurchase up to $300 million of its shares until December 2027."

The group's quarterly average dividends for the first six months of '25 were $49.3 million, or very close to its target cited above. I project JOYY's forward FY2025 dividend-to-price ratio to be 6.7%, assuming the current pace of payouts is sustained going forward.

Separately, its $0.3 billion buyback plan for FY25-27 translates into an annualized repurchases-to-market cap metric of 3.4%. At its 2Q25 analyst briefing, JOYY stressed that "we view our shares as substantially undervalued and remain committed to actively utilize buybacks."

The stock is cheap, having traded below 1x "Enterprise Value-to-Sales" or "EV/S" (source: S&P Capital IQ) in 2025 year-to-date. In my opinion, it makes sense for JOYY to continue with meaningful repurchases for the foreseeable future.

It has the capacity to reward its investors. The firm's net cash balance is a substantial $3,300 million as of the end-1H25. The monetization of its Chinese live-streaming assets for $2.1 billion was concluded in early-2025.

JOYY's potential FY25 shareholder yield (buybacks/dividends combined) is an enticing 10.1% (6.7%+3.4%).

Making Good Headway With Diversification

My end-May '24 write-up indicated that JOYY "could have introduced changes to live-streaming content in specific markets to comply with relevant local regulations," which hurts its "revenue prospects." This remains a headwind for the company.

Its livestreaming segment's top line fell 18% YoY to $0.38 billion in 2Q25, representing almost three-quarters of its group-level turnover.

JOYY took part in a conference called "CLSA Investors' Forum" in the middle of September. The organizer, CLSA, subsequently published a note (not publicly available) titled "China Internet: IF25 Wrap" detailing the takeaways on Oct. 2, 2025. Management attributed the underperformance of its core business to "content cost adjustments" and "ongoing regulatory oversight." These are the same recurring issues mentioned above.

But I find it encouraging that the enterprise has been optimizing its revenue mix.

Its proportion of sales generated from non-livestreaming operations improved from 2Q2024's 19% to 2Q25's 26%.

JOYY recently issued a press release on Oct 1, which drew attention to its "advertising platform BIGO Ads." "BIGO" was described as "one of the few" ad players globally to achieve the "IAB Tech Lab Open Measurement SDK certification for five consecutive years since 2020."

BIGO's Value Proposition

BIGO registered a +29% jump in topline to $87 million in the 2nd quarter of 2025. This implies that the advertising division will be a major driver of the company's gradual pivot away from live-streaming.

There are various signs pointing to a long growth runway for JOYY's ads business.

The firm estimates that BIGO's "Total Addressable Market" or "TAM" is $1 trillion, as per the Q2 results call disclosures. Its TAM penetration rate is under 1%, considering the annualized ad revenue of $348 million (87*4).

Also, the group guided at CLSA's event that its "third-party advertising revenue might rise over 40% YoY in 2025," which I perceive to be realistic.

JOYY noted in its Oct. 1 media release that BIGO's "publisher SDK (Software Development Kit) adoption continues to rise, with traffic increasing by nearly 80%" HoH (Half-on-Half) in 1H2025. This is a validation of the advertising unit's efforts to monetize users outside its own social media apps.

The company has suffered from sales contraction for three years (FY22-24) in a row. I'm forecasting that it will return to positive growth territory in 2H25 and FY26, considering the favorable prospects for "BIGO Ads".

You Shouldn't Ignore These Variant Views

A dividend cut or a buyback pause could lead to a lower-than-anticipated stockholder yield, which makes the stock less attractive.

JOYY's revenue downtrend might persist, in the event that macroeconomic weakness results in a decline in financial contributions from live-streaming and advertising.

My Closing Remarks

I've become positive on JOYY.

It trades at an undemanding forward EV/S of 0.66 times now. Weibo (WB), a social media/advertising peer, is valued by the market at a much richer 1.6x Enterprise Value-to-Revenue.

I see the stock re-rating on the expansion of its ads business and significant capital returns. JOYY's 10-year mean EV/S is 0.95x, or 44% (0.95/0.66-1) above its current metric.

data.title: EMQQ: Internet Technology Exposure In Emerging Markets
data.description:
The Emerging Markets Internet ETF (NYSEARCA:EMQQ) is a passively managed, thematic exchange-traded fund designed to provide investors with exposure to emerging market internet-related companies. The strategy may be utilized for growth exposure, paying out a modest distribution rate of $0.59/share, yielding 1.27% on a trailing twelve-month basis. The strategy charges a relatively high expense ratio of 86bps, well above the ETF median rate of 50bps.

About The Emerging Markets Internet ETF

EMQQ was launched on November 12, 2014 by Exchange Traded Concepts with SIE Investments Global Funds Services acting as the fund administrator. EMQQ is indexed to the EMQQ The Emerging Markets Internet Index, providing exposure to the broader Internet theme, inclusive of Internet services, retail, broadcasting, media, advertising, travel, gaming, search engines, and social networks. The Index provides for a broad investment universe, covering 47 emerging markets and frontier market nations.

The Index invests in common equities, exchange-traded American Depositary Receipts (ADRs), American Depositary Shares (ADSs), Global Depositary Receipts (GDRs), and International Depositary Receipts (IDRs). Constituents must have a market cap of a minimum of $1 billion and maintain a market cap of $800 million as part of the Index. Constituents are weighted by their float-adjusted market cap and must maintain an average daily trading volume of $1 million in share value. The Index caps constituents at a weight of 8% and is rebalanced semi-annually. The aggregate weight of constituents over 5% is limited to 50% of the total weight of the portfolio. In addition to constituents weight caps, the Index limits exposure to China and South Korea at 45% and 10%, respectively. The Index provides exposure to a total of 68 securities.

EMQQ uses a sampling methodology to gain exposure to the Index with a total of 53 holdings. Chinese and Indian equities make up a large proportion of holdings within EMQQ, accounting for 40.68% and 20.96% of net assets at the time of writing, respectively.

The strategy is heavily weighted to the top 10 holdings, making up 52.24% of net assets. In contrast, the bottom 10 holdings make up 1.65% of net assets. Top holdings include Alibaba Group Holding Limited (BABA) at 7.98%, Tencent Holdings Limited (OTCPK:TCEHY) at 7.28%, and PDD Holdings Inc. (PDD) (formerly Pinduodo) at 6.43%. These companies largely resemble American counterparts like Amazon.com Inc. (AMZN) and Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), in which Alibaba and PDD provide large-scale eCommerce while Tencent provides a wide variety of digital services, including social networks and streaming.

Who Should Consider EMQQ as an Investment Strategy

EMQQ is a thematic ETF that provides investors to emerging market securities. Though the strategy provides a modest distribution, investors should consider EMQQ for capital appreciation and growth. Investors can consider EMQQ for tactical positioning as a long-term investment strategy. Investors can also utilize EMQQ for short-term trading given its large exposure to top emerging markets technology companies.

In terms of asset allocation, EMQQ will cover emerging market equities. Given the thematic focus in Internet-related companies, EMQQ will be best considered as a satellite ETF as part of a broader diversified portfolio strategy.

EMQQ charges a relatively high expense ratio of 86bps, a modest premium to peer strategies.

EMQQ has exhibited strong long-term performance relative to peer strategies, outperforming during the 10-year historical period. The strategy has underperformed when comparing near-term metrics, potentially making lower-cost strategies like the KraneShares CSI China Internet ETF (KWEB), or one of the low-cost index funds managed by Vanguard, the Vanguard FTSE Emerging Markets Index Fund (VWO) or Schwab's Schwab Emerging Markets Equity ETFTM (SHCE) more appealing strategies for tactical positioning. KWEB will provide similar exposure to Alibaba, Tencent, and PDD while SCHE and VWO hold a position in each of these companies with a more moderate weight applied. Of course, preference around these ETFs would shift with the investment environment, when investors may prefer lower or greater concentrations to these top holdings.

Risks Related to EMQQ

EMQQ is a passively managed thematic investment strategy, exposing investors to certain risks that must be considered prior to making an investment decision. EMQQ can be considered as a concentrated strategy given the thematic exposure to Internet-related companies. Investing in emerging markets may expose investors to certain macroeconomic risks, including currency risk, inflation risk, and to a certain extent, tariff risk. EMQQ is heavily concentrated to the top 10 holdings, potentially exposing investors to concentration risk.

Final Thoughts

EMQQ is a thematic ETF designed to provide investors with exposure to emerging market Internet-based companies. The strategy provides a certain degree of concentration across its top holdings and regions, which may be appealing from a thematic approach. EMQQ will be best suited as a satellite position to gain excess exposure to the emerging market Internet theme.

This article answers these three main questions about EMQQ:

  1. What themes does EMQQ cover?
  2. How does EMQQ compare to similar ETFs?
  3. Who should own EMQQ?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Applied Digital Q1: $1 Billion NOI Is A Game Changer
data.description:
Investment Thesis

When I wrote about Applied Digital (NASDAQ:APLD) last month, I elaborated on why markets were mispricing the huge AI potential that Applied Digital would benefit from due to the rapid build-out of their data centers.

As if on cue, the company’s shares surged almost 2x over the past 30 days, since my September report, driven by a raft of structural updates in the AI infrastructure economy that are extremely constructive towards Applied’s outlook.

And I believe the shares aren’t done running yet.

Applied Digital opened their account for their fiscal year ‘26 by reporting Q1 results yesterday, and the Q1 ER was a staggering admission of how much underappreciated Applied’s shares still are. Management is projecting NOI (net operating income) of up to ~$1B in terms of an annualized run rate basis. This really changes the outlook for Applied because I don’t see any other digital infrastructure company yet growing its NOI at this rate.

I am going to reiterate my buy thesis on Applied Digital and explain my rationale below as to why yesterday’s Q1 report is pivotal for the company.

Polaris Is The Game Changing Catalyst For Applied Digital

In the Q1 FY26 report, Applied Digital reported revenues worth $64.6M, up 85% y/y, beating estimates by a massive 42%, or $19M.

Applied reiterated that all of the 400 MW of GPU capacity at its Polaris Forge 1 location in North Dakota is currently on track to being built out for its mega customer, CoreWeave (CRWV). Of that 400 MW of future capacity as shown below, 100 MW of capacity continues to be on track to come online by the end of CY25.

Management had previously guided investors separately that the CoreWeave partnership for the 400 MW of GPU capacity at Polaris Forge 1 would result in $11B in backlog for Applied. In my previous research I explained how this would translate into revenue:

Applied Digital could score ~$800-900M in revenues by 2028. That implies a revenue CAGR of ~80% over the next three years.

But what was particularly interesting to me was the speed with which Applied Digital is moving to build out its data centers.
